preamble.summary: A psalm of David. A prayer for deliverance from violent and scheming enemies whose tongues are sharp as serpents and whose lips conceal venom. The psalmist calls on the LORD as his stronghold, asks God to frustrate the plans of the wicked, and invokes judgment upon them — fire, flooding pits, and the collapse of their own violence back upon their heads. The psalm closes with a confession of faith: the LORD defends the cause of the afflicted and the poor, and the upright will dwell in His presence.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 140 is dense with body imagery — tongues sharpened like serpents, venom under lips, hands that plan violence, feet that set traps, heads surrounded by their own mischief. The enemies are described almost entirely through their body parts, each one weaponized: the tongue attacks, the lips poison, the hands scheme, the feet trap. Against this anatomy of evil, the psalmist has only one defense: the LORD as his shield and stronghold. The psalm is also notable for its serpent imagery — the tongue sharpened like a snake (shananu leshonam kemo nachash) and the venom of asps (chamat akhshuv) under the lips. The connection to Genesis 3 is implicit: the enemies are serpent-like, their weapon is speech, and their venom is concealed.
preamble.friction: The imprecations in verses 9-11 are harsh: burning coals falling on enemies, being thrown into fire, cast into floods, and hunted by violence. These are not metaphorical wishes but liturgical curses — the psalmist formally invokes divine violence against human enemies. As with Psalm 137:9, the inclusion of such language in scripture raises questions about whether these prayers are models to imitate or voices to hear. The psalm itself offers a framework: the violence of the imprecation matches the violence of the enemies' schemes. The psalmist prays that what the wicked planned for others would happen to the wicked themselves — the lex talionis applied to divine judgment.
preamble.connections: The serpent imagery connects to Genesis 3:1-5 (the serpent's deceptive speech) and Romans 3:13 (Paul quotes Psalm 140:3 as evidence of universal human corruption: 'the venom of asps is under their lips'). The 'snare' and 'net' imagery appears frequently in the Psalms (Psalm 141:9-10, 142:3). The closing affirmation that the LORD secures justice for the poor echoes Psalm 9:18, 12:5, and 72:4. The transition from Psalm 139 (God's total knowledge) to Psalm 140 (a cry against enemies) creates a logical sequence: the psalmist who invited God's searching now presents the wicked for God's scrutiny.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering: For the music director. A psalm of David.
translator_notes: The superscription assigns the psalm to David and directs it to the lamnatseach ('music director, choirmaster'), indicating liturgical performance. The Davidic attribution places the psalm in the tradition of David's many experiences of being hunted by enemies — whether Saul, Absalom, or unnamed adversaries.

verses.text_hebrew:
חַלְּצֵ֣נִי יְ֭הוָה מֵאָדָ֣ם רָ֑ע
מֵאִ֖ישׁ חֲמָסִ֣ים תִּנְצְרֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Deliver me, O LORD, from the evil man: preserve me from the violent man;
verses.rendering:
Rescue me, LORD, from the evil person;
protect me from the violent one.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with two urgent imperatives: chaltseni ('rescue me, pull me out,' from chalats, 'to draw out, to strip off, to deliver') and tintsereni ('guard me, protect me, watch over me,' from natsar, 'to keep, preserve, guard'). The enemies are described in two parallel terms: adam ra ('an evil person') and ish chamasim ('a man of violences' — the plural chamasim intensifying the charge). The word chamas means 'violence, wrongdoing, injustice' and is the same word used in Genesis 6:11 to describe the earth before the flood: 'the earth was filled with chamas.' The psalmist's enemies are described with the vocabulary of primordial corruption.
translator_notes: The verb chalats ('to rescue, to strip off, to pull out') carries the image of extracting someone from a tight place — like pulling a foot from a trap or a person from a pit. The psalmist feels caught and needs to be physically removed from danger. The plural chamasim ('violences') is an intensifying plural — not one act of violence but a pattern, a character defined by violence.

key_terms.semantic_range: violence, wrongdoing, cruelty, injustice, oppression; wrong done to others by force or fraud
key_terms.note: One of the Hebrew Bible's primary words for social evil. It encompasses both physical violence and structural injustice — force and fraud alike. In Genesis 6:11, chamas is the reason God sends the flood. The word diagnoses evil as relational injury: chamas is always done to someone.

verses.text_hebrew:
אֲשֶׁ֣ר חָשְׁב֣וּ רָע֣וֹת בְּלֵ֑ב
כׇּל־י֝֗וֹם יָג֥וּרוּ מִלְחָמֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Which imagine mischiefs in their heart; continually are they gathered together for war.
verses.rendering:
They scheme evil in their hearts;
all day long they stir up conflict.
translator_notes: The verb chashvu ('they scheme, they devise, they calculate') describes deliberate planning — these are not impulsive enemies but strategic ones. The phrase kol yom ('all day, every day') emphasizes the relentless, sustained nature of their hostility. The verb yaguru ('they stir up, they gather, they muster') with milchamot ('wars, conflicts') presents the enemies as perpetual instigators of strife.

verses.text_hebrew:
שָֽׁנְנ֣וּ לְשׁוֹנָ֣ם כְּמוֹ־נָחָ֑שׁ
חֲמַ֥ת עַ֝כְשׁ֗וּב תַּ֣חַת שְׂפָתֵ֬ימוֹ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: They have sharpened their tongues like a serpent; adders' poison is under their lips. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The first body-weapon: the tongue. The verb shananu ('they have sharpened, they have whetted') treats the tongue as a blade being honed — the serpent comparison (kemo nachash) adds the idea of a flickering, forked tongue, quick and dangerous. The second image intensifies: chamat akhshuv tachat sefateimo ('the venom of a viper under their lips'). The akhshuv is a venomous snake; its chamat ('venom, heat, poison') is hidden under the lips, ready to be delivered through speech. The enemies' words are not merely sharp but toxic — they inject poison through what they say. Paul quotes this exact verse in Romans 3:13 as part of his case that all humanity is corrupted: 'the venom of asps is under their lips.'
translator_notes: The word akhshuv is a type of venomous snake — possibly the horned viper or the cobra. The exact species is uncertain, but the point is clear: a deadly snake whose venom is concealed and delivered through a bite. The analogy is perfect for slanderous speech: the poison is hidden until the lips open.

The word selah appears here, a liturgical marker of uncertain meaning — possibly a musical interlude, a signal to pause, or an instruction to the musicians. It provides a breathing space after the intense serpent imagery.

verses.text_hebrew:
שׇׁמְרֵ֤נִי יְהוָ֨ה ׀ מִ֘ידֵ֤י רָשָׁ֗ע
מֵאִ֣ישׁ חֲמָסִ֣ים תִּנְצְרֵ֑נִי
אֲשֶׁ֥ר חָ֝שְׁב֗וּ לִדְח֥וֹת פְּעָמָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Keep me, O LORD, from the hands of the wicked; preserve me from the violent man; who have purposed to overthrow my goings.
translator_notes: The verb lidchot ('to push, to overthrow, to trip') describes the enemy's goal: to knock the psalmist off his feet, to destabilize his path. The word pe'amai ('my steps, my footfalls') represents the psalmist's life-course. The violent person schemes not merely to harm but to derail — to knock the psalmist from the path God has set.

verses.text_hebrew:
טָֽמְנוּ־גֵאִ֨ים ׀ פַּ֥ח לִי֮ וַחֲבָ֫לִ֥ים
פָּ֣רְשׂוּ רֶ֭שֶׁת לְיַד־מַעְגָּ֑ל
מֹקְשִׁ֖ים שָׁ֣תוּ לִ֬י סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: The proud have hid a snare for me, and cords; they have spread a net by the wayside; they have set gins for me. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The hunting imagery accumulates: pach ('trap, snare' — a spring-loaded device), chavalim ('cords, ropes' — for binding), reshet ('net' — for entangling), moqshim ('lures, snares' — bait-traps). The enemies are portrayed as hunters setting multiple types of traps along the psalmist's path (le-yad ma'gal, 'beside the track, at the edge of the path'). The variety of traps suggests thoroughness — every type of device has been deployed. The ge'im ('arrogant, proud ones') are the trappers. The word connects to Psalm 138:6, where God sees the proud from a distance — these are the same proud ones, now actively scheming against the righteous.
translator_notes: The four trap-words (pach, chavalim, reshet, moqshim) create an image of a path surrounded by hidden dangers. The psalmist cannot see them; only God can. This is why the prayer is necessary — the traps are hidden from human perception but visible to the all-seeing God of Psalm 139.

verses.text_hebrew:
אָמַ֣רְתִּי לַ֭יהוָה אֵ֣לִי אָ֑תָּה
הַאֲזִ֥ינָה יְ֝הוָ֗ה ק֣וֹל תַּחֲנוּנָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I said unto the LORD, Thou art my God: hear, O LORD, the voice of my supplications.
verses.rendering:
I say to the LORD, "You are my God."
Hear, LORD, the voice of my pleas for mercy.
translator_notes: The confession Eli attah ('You are my God') is personal and possessive — not 'a God' but 'my God.' The word tachanunai ('my pleas for mercy, my supplications') from the root chanan ('to be gracious, to show mercy') acknowledges that the psalmist has no leverage; he can only appeal to God's grace.

verses.text_hebrew:
יְהוִ֤ה אֲדֹנָ֗י עֹ֣ז יְ֭שׁוּעָתִי
סַכֹּ֥תָה לְ֝רֹאשִׁ֗י בְּי֣וֹם נָֽשֶׁק׃
verses.text_kjv: O GOD the Lord, the strength of my salvation, thou hast covered my head in the day of battle.
verses.rendering:
LORD God, the strength of my salvation,
You have shielded my head on the day of battle.
verses.expanded_rendering: The divine name is doubled: YHWH Adonai ('LORD Lord') — using both the covenant name and the title of sovereign authority. The phrase oz yeshu'ati ('the strength of my salvation') identifies God not merely as the source of deliverance but as its power. The verb sakkotah ('You have shielded, covered, screened') is from sakakh ('to cover, to screen, to protect') — the same root used for the weaving in the womb in Psalm 139:13. The head (roshi) is the most vulnerable part of the body in combat. The 'day of battle' (yom nasheq, 'day of weapons/armament') is the day when the enemies' schemes reach their violent climax. God has been the psalmist's helmet.
translator_notes: The connection between sakakh here (shielding the head in battle) and sakakh in Psalm 139:13 (weaving in the womb) creates a thematic link: the God who wove the psalmist's body also shields it in combat. The one who made the head protects it.

verses.text_hebrew:
אַל־תִּתֵּ֣ן יְ֭הוָה מַאֲוַיֵּ֣י רָשָׁ֑ע
זְמָמ֥וֹ אַל־תָּ֝פֵ֗ק יָר֥וּמוּ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Grant not, O LORD, the desires of the wicked: further not his wicked device; lest they exalt themselves. Selah.
verses.rendering:
Do not grant the desires of the wicked, LORD.
Do not let their schemes succeed — they would rise in triumph.
Selah.
translator_notes: The verb tafeq ('to bring forth, to promote, to advance') is rare. The psalmist asks God to block the execution of the wicked's plans. The phrase yarumu ('they would rise, they would be exalted') warns of the consequence: if God does not intervene, the wicked will be elevated — the moral order will be inverted.

verses.text_kjv: As for the head of those that compass me about, let the mischief of their own lips cover them.
verses.rendering:
As for the heads of those who surround me —
let the trouble their own lips cause cover them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The imprecation begins. The rosh mesibai ('the head/leaders of those surrounding me') are the ringleaders of the hostile group. The prayer is that their own weapon — their lips (sefateimo), the same lips that concealed venom in verse 4 — would become the instrument of their destruction. The verb yekhassemo ('let it cover them') means the trouble (amal, 'toil, trouble, mischief') generated by their own speech would engulf them. This is the lex talionis principle: the poison you prepared for others covers you instead.
translator_notes: The word amal ('trouble, toil, mischief') describes the harmful effect of the enemies' speech. The prayer that it 'cover them' reverses the direction of harm — what was aimed outward falls back on the speaker. This is a standard pattern in imprecatory psalms: the psalmist prays not for arbitrary punishment but for the reversal of the enemy's own schemes.

verses.text_hebrew:
ימיטו (יִמּ֥וֹטוּ) עֲלֵיהֶם֮ גֶּֽחָלִ֢ים
בָּ֭אֵשׁ יַפִּלֵ֑ם
בְּ֝מַהֲמֹר֗וֹת בַּל־יָקֽוּמוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let burning coals fall upon them: let them be cast into the fire; into deep pits, that they rise not up again.
translator_notes: The images escalate: gechalim ('burning coals') falling from above, esh ('fire') consuming, and mahamorot ('deep pits, flooded trenches') trapping below. The three-fold judgment attacks from every direction — above, around, and below. The phrase bal yaqumu ('let them not rise') seals the judgment as final. The burning coals echo the judgment of Sodom (Genesis 19:24) and the theophany language of Psalm 18:12-13.

verses.text_hebrew:
אִ֥ישׁ לָשׁ֗וֹן בַּל־יִ֭כּוֹן בָּאָ֑רֶץ
אִ֥ישׁ חָמָ֥ס רָ֗֝ע יְצוּדֶ֥נּוּ לְמַדְחֵפֹֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: Let not an evil speaker be established in the earth: evil shall hunt the violent man to overthrow him.
verses.rendering:
Let the slanderer find no footing in the land.
Let evil hunt the violent one, blow after blow.
verses.expanded_rendering: The ish lashon ('man of tongue' — the slanderer, the one whose weapon is speech) will not be established (bal yikkon) in the land. The verb kun ('to be established, to stand firm, to be secure') is denied — the slanderer will have no stability, no permanent place. Then the final reversal: ish chamas ra yetsudenu le-madchefot ('the violent-evil man — evil will hunt him with thrust upon thrust'). The very evil (ra) the violent person unleashed will become the hunter (yetsudenu, from tsud, 'to hunt') that pursues him. The word madchefot ('thrusts, blows, overthrows') is plural, indicating repeated strikes. Evil becomes a boomerang — what the violent person sent out comes back to hunt him.
translator_notes: The image of evil itself hunting the violent person is one of the most striking in the Psalms. Evil is personified as a predator that turns on its master. The violent person who hunted others with traps (v. 6) is now the prey. The lex talionis is cosmic: the universe itself enforces the return of violence to its source.

verses.text_hebrew:
יָ֭דַעְתִּי (יָדַ֗עְתִּי) כִּי־יַעֲשֶׂ֣ה יְ֭הוָה דִּ֣ין עָנִ֑י
מִ֝שְׁפַּ֗ט אֶבְיֹנִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: I know that the LORD will maintain the cause of the afflicted, and the right of the poor.
verses.rendering:
I know that the LORD will take up the cause of the afflicted
and the justice of the poor.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm shifts from imprecation to confidence. The verb yadati ('I know') echoes the knowledge-language of Psalm 139 — the psalmist knows something with certainty. What he knows is that the LORD ya'aseh din ani ('will execute the judgment/cause of the afflicted'). The word din ('judgment, cause, case') is legal language — God will take up their case in court. The parallel mishpat evyonim ('the justice of the poor, the legal right of the destitute') reinforces the legal framework: the afflicted and the poor have a case, and God will argue it. This is the theological foundation beneath the imprecations — the curses against the wicked are not personal vendetta but appeals to divine justice on behalf of the vulnerable.
translator_notes: The pairing of ani ('afflicted, humble, poor') and evyon ('destitute, needy') represents the most vulnerable members of society — those with no advocate, no power, no legal standing. The LORD serves as their advocate, their judge, and their defender. This is the consistent claim of the Psalms: God's court favors those whom human courts ignore.

key_terms.semantic_range: judgment, legal case, cause; of the afflicted, humble, poor, oppressed
key_terms.note: Din is a legal term — a case brought before a judge. When applied to the ani ('afflicted'), it means God takes up the legal case of those who have no other advocate. The Hebrew Bible consistently presents God as the defender of the powerless in the divine court.

verses.text_hebrew:
אַ֣ךְ צַ֭דִּיקִים יוֹד֣וּ לִשְׁמֶ֑ךָ
יֵשְׁב֥וּ יְ֝שָׁרִ֗ים אֶת־פָּנֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely the righteous shall give thanks unto thy name: the upright shall dwell in thy presence.
verses.rendering:
Surely the righteous will give thanks to Your name;
the upright will dwell in Your presence.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes with certainty: akh ('surely, certainly, indeed') introduces the final affirmation. The tsaddiqim ('righteous ones') will give thanks (yodu) to God's name — their worship is the outcome of God's justice. The yesharim ('upright, straight ones') will dwell (yeshvu) with God's face (et panekha, 'with Your presence'). The verb yeshvu ('they will sit, they will dwell') is the posture of settled belonging — not visiting but residing. The face (panim) of God is His presence — to dwell with God's face is the highest form of communion. The psalm that began with a cry for rescue ends with a vision of permanent, peaceful dwelling in the divine presence. The enemies' traps have failed; the righteous are home.
translator_notes: The movement from the psalm's opening cry ('Rescue me!') to its closing vision ('the upright will dwell in Your presence') maps the arc of the entire psalm: from danger to safety, from enemies to God, from the trap-lined path to the divine dwelling place. The verb yeshvu ('will dwell') is the same root as the 'sitting' of Psalm 137:1 ('there we sat') — but now the sitting is in God's presence, not by Babylon's rivers.
